turns.content: I'm getting a compile error in our new codebase saying that the trait bound for impl Trait is not satisfied when I try to use it as a generic argument. It seems like Rust isn't inferring the type correctly here. Can someone explain what went wrong?

The issue stems from how Rust handles generics with traits versus concrete types. When you write something like fn foo<T: Trait>(x: T), Rust requires that the caller provides a specific type instance, not just any trait object. If you were trying to pass impl Trait as a generic argument directly without wrapping it in a concrete type or using dyn Trait, the compiler cannot guarantee monomorphization because it doesn't know which concrete implementation of Trait to use at compile time. You need to either specify the exact type parameter T: Trait or use a dynamic dispatch trait object like &dyn Trait if you want runtime flexibility, though that changes how Rust compiles your function from zero-cost abstractions to dynamic calls.

Ah okay so I misunderstood and thought I could just pass any implementation of the trait as a generic argument without specifying which one. But wait, isn't monomorphization supposed to be automatic? Why does this break my code if I'm already using generics correctly? And how much does it affect binary size?

You're right that Rust aims for zero-cost abstractions, but monomorphization only works when the compiler can statically determine which version of a generic function to generate. If your code relies on dynamic dispatch via trait objects or unsafe blocks preventing full type resolution, Rust cannot create separate copies of the function for each type, so it defaults to a single dynamic dispatch implementation at runtime. This increases binary size because you're carrying the overhead of vtable lookups instead of direct calls. You can measure this impact using cargo bloat to see how much extra space generic functions with complex trait bounds add versus concrete types.
